summary: The post argues that the common startup plan to win SMBs first and then graduate to enterprise rarely works as imagined, because enterprise sales take far longer, demand different products and buying motions, and can derail a company’s model. Stancil says most startups either need a path to scale within their initial buyer segment or start as enterprise-first from day one, rather than assuming a smooth transition later.
money_quotes: But it takes much longer, happens much later, and is a much more fitful and distracting journey than almost every startup thinks that it is.
In the best cases, interminably flirting with the enterprise is psychological torture.
Whatever segment powers a startup to $10 million in revenue needs to be able to power it to $100 million.
The buyers are too different, and products and brands are too hard to change.
On a long enough timeline, any company can sell a product to both small companies and big ones. But for most startups, that timeline is well beyond a horizon that’s worth planning for.
key_insight: Assuming you can seamlessly pivot from SMB traction to enterprise revenue is a strategic error; either your initial segment must scale to $100M or you must build for enterprise from the start.
